title: DevOps Best Practices That Actually Matter

seo.title: DevOps Best Practices That Actually Matter
seo.description: The DevOps practices that move the needle: CI/CD, IaC, observability, SRE, and DORA metrics — and how Baalvion applies them across 198 markets and 180+ jurisdictions.

author: Baalvion Engineering
excerpt: DevOps is a discipline, not a job title. The practices that actually matter — CI/CD, IaC, observability, SRE, and DORA — and how Baalvion runs them across regulated, multi-tenant trade infrastructure.

blocks.text: DevOps is a discipline, not a department

The fastest way to fail at DevOps is to treat it as a hiring category — to stand up a "DevOps team" that owns the pipelines and the cloud accounts, and then let the rest of engineering throw code over the wall to it. That recreates the exact silo DevOps was invented to dissolve. DevOps is a set of practices and a culture that shorten the distance between an idea and a verified change running in production, while keeping the system safe to change. The organisational shape is downstream of that goal, not the other way around.

At Baalvion Industries we build and operate the [Baalvion Operating System](/platform), a multi-tenant trade infrastructure that moves regulated data and real money across 198 markets and 180+ jurisdictions. When a deploy can disrupt cross-border settlement or risk exposing one tenant's ledger to another, sloppy delivery is not a velocity problem — it is a solvency and compliance problem. That pressure forces clarity about which DevOps practices actually move the needle and which are cargo-culted ritual. This article covers the five that consistently matter: continuous delivery, infrastructure as code, observability, site reliability engineering, and measuring all of it with DORA metrics.
Continuous integration and delivery: small, reversible, frequent

Almost every reliability and velocity gain in DevOps traces back to one habit: integrating small changes frequently behind automated gates. Long-lived feature branches are where merge pain, integration surprises, and "works on my machine" defects accumulate. Trunk-based development with short-lived branches, merged at least daily and protected by a fast, trustworthy test suite, keeps the integration surface tiny. The pipeline then becomes a sequence of progressively expensive gates — lint, unit, contract, integration, security scan — each catching a specific class of defect at the cheapest point before it reaches a customer.

The decoupling that matters most is separating deploy from release. Deploying code to production and exposing a behaviour to users are different actions. Feature flags, dark launches, and progressive-delivery patterns — canary and blue-green — let us push code continuously while controlling blast radius. A canary routes a small slice of real traffic to the new version and watches its golden signals before promotion; if error rate or latency degrades, the rollout halts automatically. We go deeper on stage design, test pyramids, and rollback discipline in [Designing Robust CI/CD Pipelines](/news/tech/ci-cd-pipelines), but the principle is simple: every change should be small enough to reason about and reversible within minutes.

items: Trunk-based development with short-lived branches and mandatory peer review.
Automated quality gates ordered cheapest-first: lint, unit, contract, integration, end-to-end.
Deploy decoupled from release via feature flags and progressive delivery (canary, blue-green).
Immutable, signed build artifacts promoted unchanged from staging to production — never rebuilt per environment.
Automated, tested rollback as a first-class path, not a panicked manual procedure.

blocks.text: Infrastructure as code: the environment is part of the artifact

If your application is versioned and reviewed but your infrastructure is click-configured in a cloud console, you have automated half the system and left the other half to memory and tribal knowledge. Infrastructure as code closes that gap by declaring networks, clusters, databases, IAM policies, and DNS in version-controlled definitions — Terraform and OpenTofu for provisioning, with configuration managed declaratively. The unit of change is a reviewed pull request, the same workflow as application code, with a plan diff that shows exactly what will be created, modified, or destroyed before anyone approves it.

The discipline that separates real IaC from scripted clicking is immutability and reconciliation. We treat servers as cattle, not pets: instead of patching a running host, we build a new image and replace it, which makes drift impossible to accumulate silently. GitOps takes this further — the desired state lives in Git, and a controller such as Argo CD or Flux continuously reconciles the live cluster toward it, so an out-of-band manual change is detected and reverted rather than quietly becoming the new normal. For a regulated platform this is also an audit story: every infrastructure change is a signed, reviewed commit with an author and a reason. We unpack the patterns, module design, and state-management trade-offs in [Infrastructure as Code for the Enterprise](/news/tech/infrastructure-as-code), and the container-orchestration layer that sits on top in [Kubernetes for Enterprises](/news/tech/kubernetes-for-enterprises).
Observability: instrument for questions you haven't asked yet

Monitoring tells you whether the things you predicted would break are broken. Observability lets you ask new questions of a running system without shipping new code — and in distributed systems, the failures that hurt are precisely the ones nobody predicted. The practical foundation is the three telemetry signals working together: structured logs for narrative detail, metrics for aggregate trends and alerting, and distributed traces for following a single request across service boundaries. OpenTelemetry has become the vendor-neutral standard for emitting all three, which matters because it decouples instrumentation from whichever backend you analyse it in.

The mistake teams make is instrumenting volume instead of meaning. A million debug logs nobody reads is cost, not insight. We orient instrumentation around the four golden signals — latency, traffic, errors, and saturation — and around high-cardinality attributes that let us slice by tenant, market, or jurisdiction when something goes wrong. In a multi-tenant platform, the question is rarely "is the service up" but "is this tenant in this region experiencing elevated settlement latency," and you can only answer that if you instrumented for it ahead of time. Trace context propagated end-to-end is what turns a vague "the API is slow" report into a precise "a downstream FX-rate call is timing out for one corridor" diagnosis.
Site reliability engineering: error budgets turn reliability into a decision

Site reliability engineering supplies the operating model that makes "reliable" a measurable, negotiable target instead of an argument between teams. The core mechanism is the service level objective and its companion, the error budget. You define a service level indicator that reflects real user experience — say, the proportion of settlement requests served successfully under 300ms — and set an SLO, perhaps 99.9% over 30 days. The remaining 0.1% is the error budget: an explicit allowance for failure that both engineering and product agree to spend.

That single number resolves the chronic feature-velocity-versus-stability fight with data rather than opinion. When the budget is healthy, the team ships aggressively. When it is exhausted, the policy is automatic: feature work pauses and reliability work takes priority until the system is back within budget. SRE also insists on bounding toil — manual, repetitive operational work that scales linearly with growth — and reinvesting that time into automation. Blameless postmortems complete the loop: every significant incident produces a written analysis focused on the systemic and process causes that let a single mistake become an outage, never on punishing the engineer who pushed the button.

items: SLIs measured from the user's perspective, not from server-side proxies that hide real experience.
SLOs and error budgets agreed jointly by engineering and product, with an enforced burn-rate policy.
Toil capped as a percentage of operational time and systematically automated away.
Blameless postmortems with tracked action items that actually get closed.
On-call that is humane, well-tooled, and backed by runbooks — not heroics.

blocks.text: DORA metrics: measure the system, not the activity

Everything above is only as honest as your willingness to measure outcomes. The DORA metrics — from the DevOps Research and Assessment program — are the most validated way to do that, because they capture both speed and stability and correlate with organisational performance. Two measure throughput: deployment frequency (how often you ship to production) and lead time for change (how long from commit to running in production). Two measure stability: change failure rate (the proportion of deploys that cause a degradation requiring remediation) and mean time to restore service after an incident.

The reason these four work as a set is that they resist gaming. You cannot juice deployment frequency by shipping reckless changes without watching change failure rate climb. You cannot chase a low failure rate by freezing releases without lead time and deployment frequency collapsing. Together they describe a system that ships often and recovers fast — the actual definition of elite delivery performance. We resist vanity metrics like raw commit counts or lines of code, which measure activity, not value. The compliance and integrity controls that this delivery discipline protects — encryption, segregation, auditability — are detailed on our [Trust & Security](/trust) page, and the way we package all of this for clients is described under [DevOps services](/services/devops). DevOps best practices are not a checklist to complete once; they are a feedback loop you keep tightening, and DORA is how you know the loop is working.

None of these five practices is novel, and that is the point. The teams that succeed are not the ones chasing the newest tool but the ones that apply continuous delivery, infrastructure as code, observability, SRE, and DORA measurement with discipline and to each other's benefit. Small reversible changes are only safe because observability catches their side effects. Error budgets are only enforceable because you measure stability. Infrastructure as code is only trustworthy because the pipeline tests it. At Baalvion the practices reinforce one another into a single property: the ability to change a system that handles regulated money and data, confidently, many times a day, without fear.

faqs.q: Should we create a dedicated DevOps team?
faqs.a: Usually not as a gatekeeper. A central team that owns all pipelines and infrastructure recreates the silo DevOps exists to remove. A better model is platform engineering: a small team builds an internal platform — paved-road pipelines, golden IaC modules, self-service environments — that product teams consume to deploy and operate their own services with ownership but without reinventing the plumbing.

faqs.q: What are the four DORA metrics and why those four?
faqs.a: Deployment frequency, lead time for change, change failure rate, and mean time to restore. The first two measure throughput; the last two measure stability. They work as a set because they resist gaming — you cannot improve one by sacrificing another without the trade-off showing up immediately, which forces genuine system improvement rather than local optimisation.

faqs.q: What is the difference between monitoring and observability?
faqs.a: Monitoring checks predefined conditions you already anticipated — disk full, CPU high, endpoint down. Observability is the property of being able to ask new questions about a running system from its telemetry without shipping new code. In distributed systems the costly failures are the unanticipated ones, so you instrument with logs, metrics, and traces (typically via OpenTelemetry) to investigate the unknown.

faqs.q: How do error budgets actually change team behaviour?
faqs.a: An error budget converts reliability into a shared, spendable quantity. If your SLO is 99.9%, the 0.1% of allowed failure is the budget. When it is healthy, teams ship features aggressively; when it is exhausted, an agreed policy automatically prioritises reliability work over new features until the system recovers. It replaces the velocity-versus-stability argument with a data-driven decision.

faqs.q: Is infrastructure as code worth it for a small system?
faqs.a: Yes, earlier than most teams expect. The value is not only scale but reproducibility, reviewability, and auditability. Even a modest system benefits from being able to recreate its environment from a version-controlled definition, review infrastructure changes as pull requests, and detect drift. For regulated workloads it is effectively mandatory, since every change becomes a signed, attributable commit.

faqs.q: Which DevOps practice should a team adopt first?
faqs.a: Start with continuous integration and a trustworthy automated test suite, because nearly every other practice depends on it. Without fast, reliable tests you cannot safely deploy frequently, you cannot make small reversible changes, and your DORA stability metrics will be noise. Once CI is solid, layer on infrastructure as code, then observability, then SRE practices like SLOs and error budgets.
